---
title: "Practice: <Module title>"
subtitle: "Answers are hidden — try honestly first, then click to check."
engine: knitr
---

<!-- INTERACTIVE PRACTICE: the include line below wires WebR into this page so the
     learner can solve each problem in-browser on a phone. Keep it whenever any
     problem has a live `{webr}` starter (the normal case). Delete it and
     `engine: knitr` only if the whole set is static. Rules:
     .claude/course-authoring/interactive-webr.md. Do NOT add a per-file `format:`. -->
{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

<!--
TEMPLATE INSTRUCTIONS (delete all HTML comments when filling in):
- 4–7 problems on the FADING RAMP (full contract:
  .claude/course-authoring/problem-authoring.md — the single source of truth):
    P1: confidence rep — ALWAYS "redo the lesson's toy example with new numbers".
    P2: faded worked example — a worked solution with 1–2 steps blanked out; the
        learner supplies ONLY the missing steps. Blank the core move, not bookkeeping.
    P3–P4: full problems, by hand then in R; include ONE spot-the-error built from a
        lesson Common-trap (say it contains exactly one mistake).
    P5: interleaved — explicitly mixes an earlier module's skill, labeled
        "*(uses <module>'s <skill>)*". Skip only if no earlier module exists.
    Last: transfer to a new context, or a clearly-labeled cliffhanger.
- Mix by-hand and in-R. At least one problem tests INTERPRETATION, not computation.
  In-R problems keep the predict-then-run habit.
- COPY-ABLE DATA (non-negotiable): any problem that gives the learner specific data
  (a vector/function/data frame they'd otherwise retype) opens with a STARTER chunk
  defining that data — by-hand problems included. Exempt: built-in datasets and
  scalar-only prompts.
- INTERACTIVE STARTERS (see interactive-webr.md): the starter the learner solves is a
  live `{webr}` cell (`#| autorun: false` — they write before they run). The worked
  ANSWER chunk inside the callout STAYS a baked `{r}` chunk so its result shows the
  moment the callout opens. `{webr}` cells take no knitr label; keep the old
  `pN-starter` name as a leading comment. Non-WebR-runnable code stays static `{r}`
  under the "runs on your machine" callout.
- EVERY answer: inside ::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"} ... :::
  with the FULL worked reasoning — every algebraic step / every line of R explained,
  never just the final number.
- Use the same symbols + pronunciations as the lesson. No new notation here.
- Capstone modules use the cumulative format from course-structure.md §Capstone
  instead of this ramp.
-->

```{r setup}
#| include: false
# Build-time setup only (a `{webr}` starter does not see it — each starter re-states
# its own data). Packages are installed automatically (into renv; run
# renv::snapshot() afterwards if you add one).
needed <- c()
to_install <- setdiff(needed, rownames(installed.packages()))
if (length(to_install) > 0) install.packages(to_install)
invisible(lapply(needed, library, character.only = TRUE))
set.seed(42)
```

Work these in order — they get harder. Do them on paper or in an R session; only
then open the answer.

## Problem 1 — the toy example, new numbers

<!-- Restate the lesson's toy computation with fresh, equally small numbers. -->

<problem statement>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# p1-starter — edit and Run to solve, right here
x <- c(...)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<full worked steps in prose, then the same steps in a small BAKED `{r}` chunk>
:::

## Problem 2 — fill in the missing step

<!-- Faded worked example: show the worked solution with 1–2 steps replaced by
     <your step here>. The learner writes ONLY the blanked steps. -->

<problem statement, then the partial solution with the blanked step(s):>

<step 1, shown>
<step 2: **your step here** — what has to happen between step 1 and step 3?>
<step 3, shown>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# p2-starter — edit and Run to check the blank
x <- c(...)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<the missing step(s), worked in full, plus one line on why that's the move>
:::

## Problem 3 — <short descriptive name>

<problem statement — full problem, by hand or in R>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# p3-starter — your job: <what to compute>
x <- c(3, 1, 4, 1, 5)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<worked answer, including the completed R code with math-comments>
:::

## Problem 4 — spot the error

<!-- Take a Common-trap from the lesson and turn it into a short, plausible wrong
     computation (prose steps or a small chunk). Exactly one mistake; say so. -->

A colleague computed <thing> like this — it contains **exactly one mistake**. Find
it and fix it.

<the flawed computation, prose or starter chunk>

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<name the mistake, show why it's wrong with the numbers, show the corrected step>
:::

## Problem 5 — mixing it with <earlier module> *(uses <module>'s <skill>)*

<!-- Interleaved: genuinely requires the earlier skill plus this module's. -->

<problem statement>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# p5-starter — edit and Run to solve, right here
x <- c(...)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<worked answer, flagging where the earlier module's move gets used>
:::

## Problem 6 — <transfer, or a gentle cliffhanger>

<!-- New context, or a labeled preview: "you can't quite solve this yet — the next
     lesson gives you the missing tool". Cliffhangers must be labeled as such. -->

<problem statement>

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Answer"}
<worked answer — for a cliffhanger, the honest partial answer plus what's coming>
:::
